entry.title: Re: MultiLevel BOM Report for all Materials
entry.content: Yes, it is feasible in S/4HANA. You can either build a CDS view for real-time BOM hierarchy reporting or create a custom table refreshed by ABAP for stable snapshots. The choice depends on whether you need real-time analytics or periodic frozen data for budgeting.

entry.title: Re: Status of Occupational Health in SAP S/4HANA for EHS
entry.content:
Yes,
 
Occupational Health has been merged into S/4HANA EHS Workplace Safety as standard capability.
 
Therefor a customer does not need to purchase additional licensing of the previous RCS solution in S/4HANA 2025.
 
Also, as part of the roadmap for Environment Health and Safety for the S/HANA Public Cloud, a 'revamped' OH solution is planned for Q3 2026, which normally means that it should be available in two years' time for private and on premise.  OH is currently not available in Public Cloud.
 

 

Thanks!

entry.title: Cannot create Vendor with grouping B1C for existing Business Partner
entry.content:
Hi everyone, I am working on a demo version of SAP FICO and need help with a Business Partner issue. A Customer Master Record was successfully created under BP 9921 using grouping B1C, but when attempting to extend the same BP to also function as a Vendor, the following error appears: "You cannot create a vendor with grouping B1C."

Since the BP number is already linked to the Customer Master Record, the grouping cannot be changed.

  1. Is it possible to extend a BP with a customer grouping to also hold a Vendor role?
  2. Should a neutral/combined grouping have been used from the start, and if so which one supports both roles?
  3. Is there any way to reassign the grouping without losing the existing Customer Master Record data?
  4. An Accounts Receivable account was created as a workaround — was this correct or does it need to be reversed?
  5. What is the recommended process for linking both a Customer and Vendor role to the same BP number?

Any help is appreciated, thank you!

entry.title: ABAP Performance Tuning in S/4HANA Series : Part 3 : SAT + ST12
entry.content:
SAT + ST12: Profiling ABAP CPU and Memory the Right Way

Series: ABAP Performance Tuning in S/4HANA | Part 3 of 7 | Author: Manish Khanna

Your ST05 trace is clean. No expensive SQL, no N+1 patterns, indexes in place. But the program still takes 45 seconds. The problem is inside ABAP itself — a loop doing MOVE-CORRESPONDING on a 200-field structure 80,000 times, or a factory method creating a new object instance on every iteration. SAT finds the first problem in 10 minutes. ST12 finds the second.

This post covers both tools and when to use which.

SAT — Finding CPU Hotspots

Transaction: SAT (replaces legacy SE30)

SAT measures CPU time, call counts, and execution time per ABAP statement, function module, method, and form routine. Use it when the database is not the bottleneck.

Running an SAT Trace

Step 1 — Configure

Enter SAT. Click Change Measurement Settings (F6)

 

Setting Recommendation
VariantAll Statements on the first run
Aggregate records✅Enable — raw traces can be millions of rows
Maximum trace records500,000 to start; increase if trace cuts off
Restrict to userYour user ID

💡Tip: For batch jobs, use the Background measurement option. For dialog programs, use Local — traces only your session.

Step 2 — Start, Execute, Stop

Click Start Measurement (F8). Status bar shows "Measurement active". Execute your program or transaction. Return to SAT and click Stop Measurement.

Step 3 — Evaluate

Click Evaluate Last Measurement. The hit list shows rows sorted by gross time descending.

Reading SAT Results

The metric that matters: Net time percentage

Gross time includes all sub-calls. Net time is exclusive to that statement. A method with 80% gross time but 2% net time is innocent — the cost is inside a child call. Sort by net time to find the real bottleneck.

💡Tip: If a single statement consumes more than 20% of net time, investigate it first. Everything else is secondary.

Call count anomalies are often more important than per-call time:

A method at 0.01ms per call sounds harmless. At 80,000 calls, that's 800ms — significant. Look for methods with call counts in the tens of thousands, especially inside loops.

What Each Statement Type Tells You

 

Statement Type High Value Means
SELECTMove to ST05 — the DB layer is the real problem
LOOP ATCheck table type, key access, WHERE condition
CALL METHODObject instantiation inside loops, excessive polymorphism
MOVE-CORRESPONDINGLarge structures with field catalog mismatch
CONCATENATE / string opsString building in a loop — use string tables

Common SAT Findings and Fixes

MOVE-CORRESPONDING in a loop on large structures:

" ❌ Bad: MOVE-CORRESPONDING on 80-field structure, 50,000 times
LOOP AT lt_source INTO ls_source.
  MOVE-CORRESPONDING ls_source TO ls_target.
  APPEND ls_target TO lt_target.
ENDLOOP.

" ✅ Good: map only the fields you actually need
LOOP AT lt_source ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<src>).
  DATA(ls_t) = VALUE ty_target(
    vbeln = <src>-vbeln
    posnr = <src>-posnr
    matnr = <src>-matnr ).
  APPEND ls_t TO lt_target.
ENDLOOP.

String concatenation in a loop:

" ❌ Bad: CONCATENATE creates a new string object each iteration
DATA lv_result TYPE string.
LOOP AT lt_lines INTO lv_line.
  CONCATENATE lv_result lv_line INTO lv_result SEPARATED BY ','.
ENDLOOP.

" ✅ Good: build the table first, concatenate once
LOOP AT lt_lines INTO lv_line.
  APPEND lv_line TO lt_parts.
ENDLOOP.
CONCATENATE LINES OF lt_parts INTO lv_result SEPARATED BY ','.

Always use ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL in performance-critical loops:

" ❌ Bad: copies the entire structure per iteration
LOOP AT lt_data INTO ls_data.
  ls_data-status = 'X'.
  MODIFY lt_data FROM ls_data.
ENDLOOP.

" ✅ Good: direct reference, no copy
LOOP AT lt_data ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs>).
  <fs>-status = 'X'.
ENDLOOP.

ST12 — Adding Memory Analysis

Transaction: ST12

ST12 gives you everything SAT gives you, plus memory consumption tracking per object and class. Use ST12 instead of SAT whenever:

  • The program uses ABAP Objects (classes) extensively
  • You're seeing STORAGE_PARAMETERS_WRONG_SET dumps
  • Memory usage grows over time in long-running sessions

 

 

SAT vs. ST12 — When to Use Which

 

 SAT ✅ST12✅
Runtime analysis✅✅
Memory allocation tracking❌✅
Object instance counting❌✅
Trace file sizeSmallerLarger
Best forPure CPU profilingOO programs, memory problems

Running an ST12 Trace

Enter ST12. Click New Measurement (F6)

 

Option Setting✅
ABAP trace✅Always enable
Memory trace✅Enable
Aggregate identical positions✅Enable
Maximum size (KB)50,000 for most programs

Start measurement, run your program, stop. Double-click the saved measurement.

The Memory Consumption View

Click Memory Analysis. The columns to watch:

 

Column What It Tells You
Allocated (KB)Heap memory allocated by this object
Released (KB)Memory freed (GC, FREE, CLEAR)
Net allocationAllocated minus Released — net growth
InstancesNumber of object instances created

Red flags:

  • Net allocation > 10 MB for a single transaction
  • Instances count in the hundreds of thousands for a class that should have 1–2 instances
  • Net allocation grows each time you call the same transaction — accumulating without release

The Most Common Memory Anti-Patterns

Object instantiation inside a loop — the silent memory killer:

" ❌ Bad: 50,000 instances created and never GC'd efficiently
LOOP AT lt_data INTO ls_data.
  lo_processor = NEW zcl_data_processor( ).
  lo_processor->process( ls_data ).
ENDLOOP.

" ✅ Good: one instance, reused
lo_processor = NEW zcl_data_processor( ).
LOOP AT lt_data INTO ls_data.
  lo_processor->process( ls_data ).
ENDLOOP.

Static class attributes that accumulate across calls:

Static attributes persist for the entire ABAP session lifetime. If a method is called in a loop and pushes to a static cache without eviction, memory grows without bound:

" ❌ Bad: static cache grows unbounded
CLASS zcl_cache DEFINITION.
  CLASS-DATA: mt_cache TYPE HASHED TABLE OF ty_entry
                        WITH UNIQUE KEY key.
  CLASS-METHODS: add_entry IMPORTING is_entry TYPE ty_entry.
ENDCLASS.

" ✅ Good: add a maximum size + eviction policy
CLASS-METHODS: add_entry IMPORTING is_entry TYPE ty_entry.
" In implementation:
" IF lines( mt_cache ) > 10000. DELETE mt_cache ... ENDIF.

Using CL_ABAP_MEMORY_UTILITIES for programmatic monitoring:

DATA(lv_memory_kb) = cl_abap_memory_utilities=>get_current_memory_use( ) / 1024.
WRITE: / 'Current memory (KB):', lv_memory_kb.

Add this before and after the code block you're profiling to measure net growth.

The Combined Workflow

  1. Run ST05 — clean? Good, move on.
  2. Run SAT — find the high-net-time statements.
  3. If you see CALL METHOD with very high call counts, or any OO code: switch to ST12.
  4. In ST12, check both the runtime view (same as SAT) AND the memory view.
  5. Fix one thing. Re-measure. Confirm improvement before the next fix.

Never skip step 5. A change that looks like it should help sometimes makes things worse. The measurement is the proof.

Key Takeaways

  • SAT is the right tool when the database is not the bottleneck
  • Sort by net time, not gross time — gross time includes child calls
  • High call counts in loops are often more impactful than high per-call time
  • Use ST12 instead of SAT for any OO-heavy or memory-intensive program
  • Always use ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL in performance-critical LOOP AT
  • Fix one thing at a time, re-measure after each change

Next in this series: ATC: Make the ABAP Test Cockpit Your Pre-Transport Quality Gate — catch anti-patterns statically before any trace, and integrate ATC into your CI/CD pipeline.

Series Home Page

entry.title: Re: SAP Public cloud - how to activate cost center in sal...
entry.content:
Good day @nauttawanit 

Thank you for your question.

If you are referring to the standard cost center field at sales order item level,  then you cannot activate this field as a standard item field in Public Cloud in the way you would typically expect from configuration or UI activation. See KBA 3475182 for more insights.

If your goal is to capture or display cost center related information on the sales order item for business purposes, then the Public Cloud supports by allowing you to create a custom field in business sales context: Sales Document Item. Enable it for the sales order UI, and expose it through Adapt UI in Manage Sales Order -Version 2. See KBA 3619567 for more insights.

So, i recommend:

  1. You confirm whether your need is accounting driven or informational.
  2. If it is informational, then create a custom field in Sales: Sales Document Item.
  3. Enable and publish the field.
  4. Add it to the item UI in Manage Sales Orders-Version 2 and test the behaviour in a test sales order.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

As explained in this blog, this feature only supports an ABAP CDS view entity as a remote object, and there are no plans to support other objects directly. entry.title: Re: Switching from D365 FO Consultant to SAP S/4 Hana Fun...
entry.content:
I’ve actually worked on both sides. Earlier with SAP, and now I’m working with a Dynamics 365 partner, so I can relate to your situation.

Your SCM knowledge is definitely transferable. Core processes like procurement, inventory, and planning are very similar across both systems. The challenge is not capability, it’s the lack of SAP experience tag.

From what I’ve seen:
SAP is more structured and deep, but harder to break into. D365 is more flexible and easier to adapt, but SAP roles strongly prioritize prior product experience.

That’s why you’re not getting shortlisted.

If you really want to switch, focus on getting hands-on SAP exposure. Even small project experience matters more than just certifications. Also be prepared for a possible step back initially to enter the SAP ecosystem.

Once you get in, your combined D365 + SAP experience will actually become a strong advantage.

entry.title: Re: Valuation Category Enable Issue
entry.content:
Dear @venkatachalam47,

The system does not allow changing / activating split valuation (single-batch valuation) once the material has already been used in the plant if valuation-related data still exists. Even if current stock has been issued out and open documents have been closed, SAP may still block the change because valuation-related dependencies may still remain in the system, such as existing valuation types. In related Public Cloud material master checks, SAP also documents that valuated stock in the current or previous period, as well as open purchasing or scheduling dependencies can block such changes.

SAP Public Cloud documentation states that the valuation category can only be changed when the valuation type no longer exists for the material

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3682327

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3558716

Recommended solution options:

  1. Check whether any valuation type still exists for the material and, if yes, set it for deletion / archiving according to SAP guidance
  2. Verify that there is no valuated stock in either the current or the previous period and that no open purchase orders or delivery schedule lines remain.
  3. If historical dependencies cannot be fully removed the safest practical approach is to create a new material or a new plant extension and maintain the valuation setup correctly before any postings. SAP Help indicates that single-batch valuation must be prepared upfront using valuation category X Automatic Batch

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d5699934e7004d048c4801b552f3b013/289644d401a844878ce84670517dfa98.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d5699934e7004d048c4801b552f3b013/9c62e3f7092744d49021051301263e85.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d5699934e7004d048c4801b552f3b013/476cf31fd1e046a0889bc3ef8da325c3.html

Summary:

If the historical valuation dependencies cannot be fully removed the practical workaround is to create a new material or a new plant extension and maintain the valuation setup correctly before any postings. This is an implementation recommendation based on SAP documented restrictions, not a direct SAP sentence. SAP documents that the valuation category can only be changed if the valuation type no longer exists and SAP Help shows that single batch valuation must be prepared with valuation category X an initial valuation type and dependent valuation records before inventory posting

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Shipping Invoices, Special Offers and Other Inserts
entry.content:
In most European EPRs printed paper is excluded from EPR reporting.

Items, such as invoices, leaflets, discount coupons, user manuals, magazines, etc. are not considered packaging.

However, the USA Oregon EPR includes printed paper in the scope of what needs to be reported.

The obligations of who needs to report the printed paper are described in the reporting guidance.

RDP has one packaging type, PRP, which is meant to be used for packaging elements made of printed paper.

All SAP-provided reports for Europe have a rule to exclude this packaging type.

If the company will be reporting only in Europe then it doesn't matter if they maintain the coupons and invoices as packaging elements or not.

If the company will report in USA Oregon, then they have to create the printed paper as packaging element.

They may need to adjust the rules from the delivered Oregon template, as the printed paper must be split into several categories.

Even if the company operates only in Europe, it may still decide to create the packaging elements PRP, in case future regulation changes the reporting or if they want to do internal reporting (e.g how much paper do we put on the market for our leaflets, user manuals and discount coupons).

A sustainability-engaged company may want to track these weights and make a case for digitizing the user manuals, measure the positive environmental impact of electronic invoices, etc.

Bottom lines:

  • It's hard to give a general answer - it's best for the company to check the guidance of the respective PRO
  • SAP-provided reports make sure to exclude the printed paper, wherever it is required to be excluded. This is documented in the reporting documentation.

entry.title: iDoc monitoring in Cloud ALM, status changes
entry.content:
Hello,
We are currently implementing monitoring with SAP Cloud ALM and we are focusing on Integration & Exception Monitoring because we have some essential iDoc integration flows that we need to monitor.
So we have a few questions about the capabilities of Cloud ALM in terms of being an effective real time monitoring tool to face some exeptions.
1. Is it possibile to see iDoc content in Cloud ALM?
2. We have an Outbound iDoc flow that starts from S4 (successful status 03), but we are more interested on the response we receive from BTP when the message arrives (successful status 06, error status 05).
We saw that some messages that on S4 result to have status 06, on ALM they result to still have status 03. So we need to know if that is an effective limitation or if we can assure that the status can be monitored and trigger and alert in case of error (status 05).

3. Also about the topic above, we also are interested in triggering an alert if an iDoc remains in status 03 for a long time, without a response from BTP (status 05 or 06)

Thank you

entry.title: Re: Maintain/Update Multilingual Texts in Equipment/Funct...
entry.content:
Hello @AbhijitGoswami

A slight addition/correction is that, the migration objects involved are PM-Equipment and PM-Functional Location, but migration objects are primarily for initial data migration. So, this is not the preferred method for you to maintain recurring multilingual text after going live.

See the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d5699934e7004d048c4801b552f3b013/20607b6de19a4d34a41b0abec6186b01.html?locale=en-US

For that situation, the recommendation is for you to:

  • Manually maintain the description by logging on in each required language and saving the text
  • Use API_EQUIPMENT (SAP_COM_0395) and API_FUNCTIONALLOCATION. (SAP_COM_0395)  

Note that these descriptions are language dependent and when you read them by API, the request should contain the correct language header. See KBA 3394360 for more insights.

So, the recommendation is for your to

  • Manually test one Equipment and one Functional Location by changing the Fiori logon language.
  • Confirm the language specific text behaviour in the app.
  • Make use of the text operations for API_EQUIPMENT and API_FUNCTIONALLOCATION.
  • When you validate in Postman, always pass the expected language header.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Integrating a Custom AI Model into SAP CAP Using AI Launchpad
entry.content:
As we all know AI is spreading across industries like wildfire and a lot of companies want to bring their own custom models into their SAP setups. In this blog I will walk you through a hands-on example of deploying a custom AI model to SAP AI Core. And then making it a callable service in SAP AI Launchpad and connecting it directly to a CAP backend.

Rather than relying on pre-built generative AI services, we demonstrate how to bring your own model, package it in Docker, register it in AI Launchpad, and call it from your backend using a deployment ID. The goal is to cover the whole journey from serving the model to getting real-time results right in your UI.

Requirements

If you are not running everything locally, you'll need admin roles for SAP AI Launchpad and AI Core. For a local setup, there's a build step. For running the trained model in Docker a GPU is recommended but not strictly mandatory. (A CPU will work, just slower) Make sure your machine has enough CPU power, memory and disk space. If you want GPU acceleration, you’ll need a compatible NVIDIA GPU, the right drivers, and CUDA support.

Getting Data Set For Model Training

You can grab image datasets from Roboflow, Kaggle, Google Open Images, or other publicly available sources. But if you plan to use your model for more than just experimenting, you have to check the licenses and usage rights carefully. Or just make your own dataset by collecting and labeling images for your actual use case. That way you’ve got control over both quality and compliance.

Running the Application Locally

The application is first run locally to verify that it works as expected after pushing the model to Docker.

The Dockerfile used for local testing

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

In the CAP application, the requires section in package.json is updated as follows:

"cds": {
  "sql": {
  },
  "requires": {
    "[production]": {
    },
    "[hybrid]": {
    },
    "barcode-ai": {
      "kind": "rest",
      "credentials": {
        "url": "http://localhost:8080",
        "requestTimeout": 300000
      }
    }
  },
  "auth": "xsuaa",
  "html5-runtime": true,
  "portal": true
},

On the UI side, users will upload barcode images through a FileUploader component.

<u:FileUploader
                    id="imageUploader"
                    name="myFileUpload"
                    fileType="jpg,jpeg,png"
                    placeholder="{i18n>selectBarcodeImg}"
                    change="onImageSelected"
                    width="300px" />

                <Button
                    text="{i18n>barcodeImgTrigger}"
                    type="Emphasized"
                    press="onAnalyzeBarcode"
                    class="sapUiMediumMarginTop" />

The controller then adds the uploaded image to a FormData object and fires off a POST request.

onAnalyzeBarcode: async function () {
      if (!this._selectedFile) {
        MessageToast.show("Test: Please Select An Image");
        return;
      }

      try {
        this.getView().setBusy(true);
        const formData = new FormData();
        formData.append("image", this._selectedFile);

        const response = await fetch(
          "/catalog/RoleSearchService/analyze-barcode",
          {
            method: "POST",
            body: formData
          }
        );

        if (!response.ok) {
          throw new Error("Test: Cant Call Ai Srv");
        }

        const result = await response.json();
        this.byId("resultText").setText(
          JSON.stringify(result, null, 2)
        );
      } catch (err) {
        MessageBox.error("Test: Barcode Srv Failed");
        console.error(err);
      } finally {
        this.getView().setBusy(false);
      }
    }

For local testing the service call in service.js points to your locally running API. With this end-to-end local test you can check if the API can spot barcodes in uploaded images. And if so, pull out their locations, values, types and confidence scores.

The request-response structure of the local test service

 

AI Launchpad Connections

Once you've pushed your project that includes the Dockerfile, serve.py, requirements.txt, and serving_template.yaml to the GitHub, head over to SAP AI Launchpad.

Application settings in AI Launchpad for local testing

Under SAP AI Core Administration, go to Git Repositories and add your GitHub URL. Once the repository is synced, open All Applications and then create a new application. Pick your repository, set the path to (.), and revision to (main).

With the application synced, move to ML Operations and open Scenarios. You will see the serving_template.yaml is picked up automatically as an executable. The template defines the Docker image to run, the container port, resource requests, and the imagePullSecret pointing to our Docker Hub credentials, which are registered separately under Docker Registry Secrets.

 

 

Next, make a Configuration under the scenario, linking the executable to a resource group.

One important detail here is that the serving_template.yaml must define the metadata annotations, labels, and spec fields as pipe-delimited strings rather than nested YAML objects. This is a requirement of the SAP AI Core ServingTemplate schema that differs from standard Kubernetes manifests.

apiVersion: ai.sap.com/v1alpha1
kind: ServingTemplate
metadata:
  name: barcode-reader-serving
  namespace: <your-namespace>
  labels:
    scenarios.ai.sap.com/id: "barcode-scenario"
    ai.sap.com/version: "1.0.0"
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        autoscaling.knative.dev/metric: rps
        autoscaling.knative.dev/target: "100"
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: kserve-container
          image: "<your-dockerhub-username>/barcode-reader:latest"
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8080
              protocol: TCP
          command: ["uvicorn", "serve:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8080"]
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "0.5"
              memory: "512Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "1"
              memory: "1Gi"

 

Once your configuration is set trigger a Deployment. Use the infer.m resource plan, as it gives enough compute. After a few minutes the deployment will reach Running status with a unique Deployment ID.

Deployment Details in SAP AI Launchpad

 


Model binding in the updated package.json

Take that Deployment ID and put it into the cds.requires section of package.json under the barcode-ai destination. Now you can pass base64-encoded images in the request and get back barcode predictions from your CAP service layer.

 

 

 

 

 

onAnalyzeBarcode: async function () {
      if (!this._selectedFile) {
        MessageToast.show("Please choose an image first!");
        return;
      }
      try {
        this.getView().setBusy(true);
        const arrayBuffer = await this._selectedFile.arrayBuffer();
        const base64 = btoa(
          new Uint8Array(arrayBuffer)
            .reduce((data, byte) => data + String.fromCharCode(byte), '')
        );
        const response = await fetch("/catalog/SIAService/analyzeBarcodeImage", {
          method: "POST",
          headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
          body: JSON.stringify({ image: base64 })
        });
        if (!response.ok) {
          throw new Error("Error in Ai srv call");
        }
        const raw = await response.json();
        const result = typeof raw.value === 'string' ? JSON.parse(raw.value) : raw.value;

        if (!result.barcodeValue) {
          this.byId("resultText").setText("No barcode detected in the image");
          return;
        }

        this.byId("resultText").setText(
          `Barcode: ${result.barcodeValue} | Type: ${result.confidence || "-"}`
        );

        MessageToast.show("Barcode Scanned Successfully!");

      } catch (err) {
        MessageBox.error("Barcode Analysis Failed: " + err.message);
        console.error(err);
      } finally {
        this.getView().setBusy(false);
      }
    }

 

this.on('analyzeBarcodeImage', async (req) => {
      try {
        const image = req.data.image;
        if (!image) {
          req.error(400, 'Image is required');
          return;
        }

        const barcodeAI = await cds.connect.to('<Your_Destination>');
        const deploymentId = '<deploymentId>';
        const response = await barcodeAI.send({
          method: 'POST',
          path: `/v2/inference/deployments/${deploymentId}/v2/models/barcode-model/infer`,
          headers: {
            'Content-Type': 'application/json',
            'AI-Resource-Group': 'default'
          },
          data: {
            image: image
          }
        });

        // response.predictions → [{data, type, rect}]
        const predictions = response?.predictions || [];

        if (predictions.length === 0) {
          return JSON.stringify({ barcodeValue: null, confidence: null, message: 'Barcode not found' });
        }

        return JSON.stringify({
          barcodeValue: predictions[0].data,
          confidence: predictions[0].type,
          allResults: predictions
        });

      } catch (err) {
        console.error('analyzeBarcodeImage error:', err);
        req.error(500, 'Barcode inference failed');
      }
    });

 

Once we have everything wired up and the CAP app is running, we head back to the frontend and run our test by uploading two images, one with a barcode and one without.

Barcode Detection In Action Test1

Barcode Detection In Action Test2

Future Improvements

The big takeaway integrating custom models with AI Launchpad is straightforward. And once you’ve plugged it in, you can use it across all kinds of projects with remarkable ease. That smooth integration makes a huge difference.

You can level this up in a bunch of ways. For example fine-tune a model like YOLOv8 for tricky conditions like dim lighting or angled shots or add QR code support with minimal changes or handle damaged barcodes by searching for similar visual embeddings in a database.

Summary

This blog walked through the full integration path of deploying a custom AI model to SAP AI Core and consuming it from a CAP backend. Instead of relying on a managed generative AI service, the model was packaged in Docker, registered in SAP AI Launchpad via a serving template, and called from the CAP backend using a deployment ID over the AI Launchpad Destination.

The result is a working barcode detection feature that runs entirely on SAP infrastructure, with the model hosted and scaled by AI Core and the business logic kept cleanly within the CAP service layer. And as we covered, this is just the start. The architecture is flexible. Fine-tune for complex real world scenarios, tack on QR codes, or deal with scenarios like damaged barcodes using similarity search.

Hopefully, this gives consultants a clear roadmap for moving past generic AI tools and plugging their own models into real world solutions. You're not limited to barcodes either. This blueprint works for custom vision models, language models, whatever you need.

If you have any questions or thoughts, feel free to leave a comment below.

Ege Aksöyek

entry.title: Re: ABAP Performance Tuning in S/4HANA Series : Part 2 : The ST05 Playbook
entry.content:
With HANA Database, "FOR ALL ENTRIES" is very bad in terms of performance. Better use AMDP method or join query like :

IF lt_orders IS NOT INITIAL.
  SELECT vbeln, posnr, matnr, kwmeng
    FROM vbap
    WHERE ( vbeln ) IN ( SELECT vbeln from @LT_orders as key )
    INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_items).
ENDIF.

entry.title: SAP Cloud Connector - Fix for OAuth Token Retrieval on OnPrem Systems
entry.content:
👉👉👉 PLEASE upvote for this solution with this  improvement request 👈👈👈

The Problem

Calling non-SAP APIs thorugh SAP Cloud Connector often fails when Oauth 2.0 tokens are involved. Calling APIs with OAuth authorizaition requires two steps:

  1. Get the OAuth-Token from an Oauth-Token-URL
  2. Calling the actual API using the previously obtained Oauth-Token

Obtaining the OAuth-Token through SAP Cloud Connector works as expected. The token is retrieved and can be used for the subsequent API call. However, the actual API fails stating the token is invalid. The reason for this is as follows:

When calling an Oauth-Token URL through SAP Cloud Connector, it adds the port at the end of the hostname. Example:

Actual Token URL:
https://internal.company-xyz.com/oauth/token

Token URL called by SAP Cloud Connector:
https://internal.company-xyz.com:443/oauth/token

The retrieved JWT-token now includes port 443 in the ISS field as we can see here:

{...
"iss":"https://internal.company-xyz.com:443/oauth/token"
...}


What happens now is that the subsequent API call fails because the API-backend compares the expected string
https://internal.company-xyz.com/oauth/token
against the actual value in the token, which is
https://internal.company-xyz.com:443/oauth/token

One might argue, that this is purely a API-backend problem. Technically, this is correct. Real life looks different though. Most API developers simply implement OAuth verification against the obvious issuer URL in the ISS field and do not even consider to also verify the same URL with a port at the end of the hostname.

The Solution

In case of Oauth-Token-URL system mappings in the SAP Cloud Connector, the HTTPS protocol is chosen. The solution is straight forward: In case HTTPS protocol is chosen, the Internal Port field is no longer mandatory. If left emtpy, no port will be added to the hostname when forwarding the call to the backend system.

Result:
The obtained token will not have the port number after the hostname in the ISS field within the token. Token validation by the API backend system will then be successful. The ISS field in the token now looks like:

{...
"iss":"https://internal.company-xyz.com/oauth/token"
...}

  

👉👉👉 PLEASE upvote for this solution with this  improvement request 👈👈👈

entry.title: The 3,000-Year-Old Argument: What SAP’s Business Data Cloud Actually Changes
entry.content:
20 years ago, I was sitting in the Rosengarten Congress Center in Mannheim, listening to Hasso Plattner make an argument I have never forgotten.

It was SAP’s yearly Developer Kickoff Meeting (DKOM), 2005. Hasso walked to a whiteboard and drew three boxes: Purchase Order. Goods Receipt. Invoice. Then Hasso connected them with arrows and said, in effect: merchants in ancient Mesopotamia recorded the process in clay tablet accounting ledgers. Phoenician traders did it on papyrus. The core procure‑to‑pay pattern hasn’t changed in thousands of years. We’ve been rebuilding the plumbing to execute it for decades. The process hasn’t changed. Only the vessel has.

That point wasn’t nostalgic. The argument was architectural. SAP’s job, Hasso argued, wasn’t to reinvent the business logic of the Three-Way Match. It was to give that ancient, stable logic a better vessel: faster, more flexible, reachable by more systems, at greater scale. The business process is eternal. The technology that carries it isn’t.

I remember thinking: the argument holds across every era. Gutenberg’s press didn’t change what a manuscript said. It changed the vessel: from a monk copying by hand, to movable type, to the offset press, to the screen you’re reading this on now. Same knowledge. Much better vessel.

20 years later, SAP is making that argument again, this time with Business Data Cloud (BDC), a Knowledge Graph, and a new generation of AI agents. And this time, something genuinely different is happening. Not just a better vessel. A vessel that has started to think.

 

The Arc, Era by Era

To understand what is different this time, you need to trace what changed at each stage, and more importantly, what value it unlocked for the business.

The 1970s, R/1 and R/2. SAP was founded in 1972. R/2 arrived as a full mainframe suite by the late 1970s. The procure-to-pay logic didn’t change. It was automated and, for the first time, integrated. Finance, materials management, and operations ran against a single shared data model in real time. What previously needed rooms of clerks reconciling paper accounting ledgers could now run across thousands of transactions at scale. The business logic was the same. The scale and the integration weren’t.

The early 1990s, R/3. Client/server architecture changed the plane of standardization. A company could now run the same procure-to-pay process in Brazil, Germany, and Singapore against the same master data and the same chart of accounts. The Three-Way Match became a global process, not a local one. The structural unlock was significant: global process standardization at a scale that mainframe architecture couldn’t reach.

The mid-2000s, Enterprise Services Architecture (ESA). That was the era Hasso was describing that day in Mannheim. ESA moved SAP from mostly proprietary integration toward model-driven services. Other systems could now call the Three-Way Match, not just run it inside R/3. Partners and adjacent applications could participate in the same process through documented service interfaces. The value was connectivity: SAP stopped being a closed transaction system and became an integration hub.

The 2010s, SAP HANA. Batch processing was always the structural constraint on real-time decision-making. Overnight runs, yesterday’s numbers, reconciliations that happened after the fact. SAP HANA made real-time or on demand analysis of live transaction data practical at scale. The batch job didn’t disappear, but it stopped being the only architectural option. A CFO could see the actual cash position at this moment, not last night’s approximation. That changed what finance teams were able to do, not just how fast they did it.

 

What SAP Business Data Cloud Is Changing

Each of those waves changed how the process was executed. Faster, more connected, more integrated, more real-time. None of them changed what the system understood about the process.

That distinction matters for Business Data Cloud and the Knowledge Graph.

When S/4HANA processes a Three-Way Match in real time, it knows the invoice number, the vendor ID, the amount, and whether the quantities reconcile. It executes the logic.

It doesn’t know this vendor is also a single-source supplier for a component on a three-week lead time. It doesn’t know this invoice is one of 47 from a cluster showing a dispute pattern that procurement flagged last quarter. It doesn’t know that delaying payment on this specific invoice damages a supplier relationship that affects Q3 fill rates. That knowledge exists inside the organization. It lives in the heads of the procurement manager, the CFO, and the risk analyst. But the system has never had access to it, because business context was never machine readable.

The SAP Knowledge Graph is the first systematic effort to change that. It encodes not just the objects of business, but the relationships between them: a vendor that is linked to its purchase orders, its contracts, its delivery history, its position in the supply chain network, and the risk signals that have accumulated over years. Built on decades of SAP business object modeling, it spans on the order of hundreds of thousands of tables and tens of thousands of Core Data Services (CDS) views. In SAP S/4HANA alone, the model covers millions of fields, representing 50 years of business process logic encoded not just as transactions but as a semantic network of meaning.

Customers extend this foundation with their own data and their own relationships, using tools in SAP Datasphere designed to be approachable for domain experts, not just data engineers. The SAP layer covers what only SAP could build. The customer layer covers what only the customer knows. The two are meant to form a unified view of the business that an AI agent can reason over.

This matters more than it sounds. Without the semantic layer, the failure mode of AI is specific and serious. An AI model trained or operating on disconnected enterprise data will learn that late invoices are a risk. It won’t learn this cluster of 47 invoices from a single-source supplier, combined with a three-week component lead time and a Q3 delivery dependency, is the signal that needs intervention today. The compound pattern is only visible when the entities and their relationships are connected. Without the semantic layer, AI sees individual facts. With it, AI sees the business. Data lakes aggregate. ERP systems transact. AI predicts. The Knowledge Graph defines what is connected, what is allowed, and what is true.

AI agents build on this foundation. Today they function as smart copilots: decision support, restricted workflow orchestration, surfacing the anomaly, proposing the action, routing to the right approver. The fuller vision is an agent that identifies the invoice dispute and handles it end-to-end: collaborating with collections, engaging the customer relationship, closing the situation across finance, procurement, and service without a human stitching each step together. Copilots today. Autonomous agents are next.

Here’s what the history of each platform shift teaches: the customers who extract the most value are the ones who invest with clarity. They know what the platform gives on day one, what they need to bring themselves, and where the road map is taking them over the next 3 to 5 years. BDC is no different. The architecture is sound and the direction is right. The more clearly you understand what SAP has built and what your organization needs to contribute, the more value you’ll extract.

 

The Question Worth Sitting with Before You Commit

Here’s what’s new this time.

Every previous wave gave the ancient logic a better vessel. A faster vessel. A more connected vessel. A vessel that could operate at a global scale or in real time. But the vessel always carried the logic. It executed the Three-Way Match, never understanding it.

For the first time, the vessel is starting to reason. The Knowledge Graph makes it possible for a system to know that this invoice matters not just because the amounts reconcile, but because of everything it connects to - the supplier relationship, the supply chain exposure, the cash flow position, the dispute history. That context, previously trapped in a procurement manager’s memory and a CFO’s intuition, becomes queryable, auditable, and available to an AI agent at the moment of decision.

What the vessel needs from you, is the context that SAP can’t build for you. The Knowledge Graph covers the standard business object relationships that 50 years of SAP experience can encode. Your organization’s specific vendor relationships, your risk thresholds, your definitions of what matters: those specifics live in your Customer-Managed Data Products, built with your domain expertise using the tools SAP offers. The platform is the foundation. What you build on top is where your organization differentiates, and for the first time, that organizational knowledge can be encoded at machine scale and reasoned over by AI.

Hasso’s argument in 2005 was simple: the process is eternal, the technology is the vessel, and SAP’s job is to give you a better one.

The vessel this time has something the others didn’t. It’s starting to understand what it carries.

The vessel is ready to reason. The question worth sitting with is what you’ll give it to think about.

entry.title: Re: Cannot create Vendor with grouping B1C for existing B...
entry.content:
1. Why the error occurs
The BP grouping determines the number range and type of BP you’re creating. Grouping B1C is defined only for customers in your system. When you try to extend the same BP to a vendor role, the system checks the grouping and rejects it because B1C is not allowed for vendor creation.

2. Can you extend a Customer BP to Vendor?
Yes, in principle a single BP can hold multiple roles (Customer, Vendor, Contact Person, etc.). But only if the grouping used supports both roles. If you start with a grouping restricted to customers, you cannot later add vendor role without error.

3. Neutral/Combined Grouping
SAP delivers general groupings (often called “0001” or “BP General”) that are neutral and allow both customer and vendor roles. Best practice: use a combined grouping from the start if you know the BP will need both roles. This way, the same BP number can be extended to Customer and Vendor without conflict.

4. Can You Reassign Grouping?
No, grouping cannot be changed once the BP is created. The number range and grouping are fixed. If you need both roles and the BP was created with a customer-only grouping, you must either create a new BP with a neutral grouping and assign both roles, or keep the existing BP for customer role and create a separate BP for vendor role, then link them (less ideal).

5. Accounts Receivable Workaround
Creating an AR account is not a substitute for vendor role. If you need the BP to act as a vendor (for procurement, payments, etc.), you must have the vendor role assigned. The AR account workaround should be reversed if it was only meant to bypass the BP error.

6. Recommended Process and Best practice:

  • Use a neutral BP grouping that supports both customer and vendor roles.
  • Create the BP once, then extend it to both roles.
  • If already created with customer-only grouping, you will need to create a new BP with the correct grouping for demo purposes.

entry.title: Governance: The Word Everyone Uses and Nobody Agrees On
entry.content:
We're in a strange moment for governance.

The EU AI Act is moving into enforcement. Enterprises are racing to deploy generative AI while their compliance teams scramble to figure out what "responsible use" even means in practice.

And in the middle of all this, everyone keeps using the word "governance" as if we all agree on what it means.

We don't.

If I put your data team, your AI team, and your SAP team in a room and asked them all to define "governance," I'd get at least three different answers. Your data team would talk about quality, lineage, and stewardship. Your AI team would talk about fairness, model risk, and responsible use. Your SAP team would talk about master data workflows, golden records, and change approvals.

They're all right. And they're all incomplete.

Governance isn't a single discipline. It's a spectrum. And organizations that treat it as one fragmented program after another, each with its own framework, committee, and slide deck, keep reinventing the wheel.

Here's the position I want to stake out: the governance problem isn't a capability gap. It's a visibility gap. Most enterprises already have 70% of what they need. They just can't see how the pieces connect.

Data Governance and AI Governance: Two Questions, One Continuum

Data governance answers a foundational question: can we trust our data?

That means defining ownership, measuring quality, controlling access, and tracking how data flows across systems. Most mature organizations have the building blocks: stewards, quality rules, audit trails, access controls.

AI governance picks up where data governance stops, but asks a different question: can we trust what the system does with the data?

This is the shift from managing information to managing decisions. AI governance introduces concerns that data governance was never designed to handle: bias testing, human-in-the-loop design, drift monitoring, and output validation. The accountability surface grows because the impact surface grows.

But here's what most organizations get wrong: they treat these as separate programs. The smarter approach is to recognize that most data governance capabilities are already AI governance capabilities, applied to a different lifecycle stage.

Data quality management builds trust in model performance. Lineage tracking helps surface bias. Access controls define boundaries for ethical use. Auditability enables defensible decision-making.

Same muscles. Different emphasis: inputs versus outputs.

SAP MDG: The Operational Governance Layer Most AI Discussions Miss

This is where the conversation gets practical, and where I want to challenge a blind spot.

SAP Master Data Governance is the operational execution layer. It's where governance policies become enforceable processes, embedded in the workflows your business runs on every day.

When someone wants to create a new vendor record, MDG defines who requests it, who reviews it, what quality checks it passes through, and who approves it before it reaches your production system. After a merger, MDG provides the framework to match, merge, and harmonize records at scale. These aren't administrative niceties. They're governance you can touch.

And here's the connection most people miss: SAP MDG is foundational AI governance that nobody labels as such.

A duplicate vendor record isn't just an ERP nuisance. It propagates into your analytics layer, your spend analysis models, and your AI-driven procurement recommendations. A miscategorized material doesn't just break reporting. It trains your demand forecasting model on flawed assumptions. When MDG enforces a validation rule or rejects a poorly formed record, it is protecting the integrity of the data that AI systems will eventually consume.

This is governance work happening before any model gets involved.

I've seen this disconnect firsthand. A company spends years implementing MDG with robust workflows, quality rules, and stewardship models. Then a separate team launches an AI governance initiative from a blank whiteboard, completely unaware that the operational backbone they need is already running. The disconnect is organizational, not technical. Bridging it is one of the highest-value, lowest-cost improvements most SAP customers can make.

Where SAP Business Data Cloud Closes the Loop

SAP Business Data Cloud takes this further. By exposing SAP's business data as semantically rich, governed, AI-ready data products, it creates the layer where master data quality, business context, and AI consumption connect in one governed structure.

The governance lineage becomes continuous: MDG enforces quality at the source; SAP BDC ensures that what reaches the AI layer is semantically meaningful, access-controlled, and auditable. The questions "is the record clean?" and "is the AI reasoning over trustworthy data?" finally have the same answer, because they share the same governed foundation.

SAP customers building toward enterprise AI don't need to start their governance story from scratch. The architecture is already pointing in the right direction.

The Bottom Line

Governance is elusive because we treat it as a collection of separate problems. Data governance asks whether the data is correct. AI governance asks whether the outcome is acceptable. Master data governance asks whether the record is clean and approved.

These aren't competing questions. They're complementary layers of the same capability: making trustworthy, accountable, defensible decisions at scale.

The organizations that get this right won't have the fanciest frameworks. They'll be the ones that see governance as a single connected spectrum, recognize that most of the pieces are already in place, and have the clarity to wire them together.

Governance isn't a framework problem. It's a visibility problem. And for most SAP customers, the infrastructure to solve it is closer than they think.

entry.title: Using SAP Advanced Financial Closing to Schedule Jobs For Financial Consolidation
entry.content:
This blog briefly outlines how customers can use the SAP Advanced Financial Closing solution to schedule jobs when performing financial consolidations.  For SAP Cloud ERP customers, this typically means also utilizing the group reporting consolidations functionality.

SAP Advanced Financial Closing is SAP’s strategic cloud-based orchestration and intelligent automation hub for record to report, allowing customers to automate and shorten the legal entity close cycle times while also offering superb planning, automation and monitoring for the financial close. 

One question often asked by customers that came up again during the recent February release highlights webinar was can SAP Advanced Financial Closing be used to automate jobs when performing consolidations?

The answer is yes for specific scenarios as outlined in the SAP note 3716576 or in the help documentation. For example, SAP SE is using both SAP Advanced Financial Closing and group reporting and is managing several group reporting jobs from within SAP Advanced Financial Closing during the quarter end close. 

SAP SE is using standard SAP Advanced Financial Closing functionality where a job task type executes the group reporting programs with parameters sourced from both the task model template (static/variant configuration) and the Header (dynamic values like fiscal year and period).

The integration is implemented through a task list model containing three group reporting tasks, each configured with a separate task model (Data Monitor tasks: Release Universal Journal, Calculation of Retained Earnings, Currency Translation), where parameter substitution automatically passes runtime values from the close template into the backend job variants during execution.

With a small amount of backend system set-up, you can leverage SAP Advanced Financial Closing’s built-in orchestration and parameter mapping capabilities to coordinate multiple consolidation processes within a single financial close workflow. 

The jobs Universal Journal Release: FINCS_DATA_RELEASE_TASK_JOB, Retained Earnings Calculation: FINCS_ANI_ANI00, and Currency Translation: FINCS_CTR_CTR00 are run in defined intervals. Executions follow a strict timetable with authorized users assigned. This setup ensures timely and consistent results, reduces manual effort, and safeguards data quality for follow-on processes. The solution supports repeat runs and is fully embedded in SAP Advanced Financial Closing.

So, in conclusion, SAP SE is another example of how customers are using SAP Advanced Financial Closing to automate different recurring tasks within their accounting and reporting function.  Above, we have shown examples of using SAP Advanced Financial Closing to manage jobs within SAP Cloud ERP, group reporting to reduce manual effort and improve accuracy of the financial close.  For more details, please consult note 3716576 or the help documentation.

entry.title: Re: ME21N Enhancement - Disable/Grey Out Custom Subscreen...
entry.content: Implement your dynamic control in FIELDSELECTION_HEADER of BAdI ME_GUI_PO_CUST. Check BSART, then set the field status accordingly. This ensures your custom field behaves correctly across ME21N, ME22N, and ME23N.

entry.title: Re: Inclusion of field Requisitioner in Production Operat...
entry.content:
Unfortunately, the logic MM_PUR_S4_PR_MODIFY_ITEM does not include the PURREQNSSPREQUESTOR field in its changing parameters. Therefore, it was not possible to modify this field here. 

However, we did find a workaround to achieve our goal:

We extended the logic MM_PUR_S4_PR_CHECK to exclude purchase requisitions created by Production Operations by excluding PRs with creation indicator: Production Order (F) & material group: External Work (7000) in the logic.

Therefore, these PRs will not raise an error anymore.

entry.title: Use case: Event-Driven Integration of Robotic Tire Palletizing with Cimcorp, MES, and SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
Author: Naveen Chandra Sharma

(Naveen is a SAP Solution architect having 29+ years of experience which includes 20+ years into SAP consulting with expertise in SAP ECC, SAP S/4HANA and integration with other external system like MES, CIMPRO etc. He worked on various type of project flavors like Implementation, Enhancement and support projects in various industries Manufacturing, Finance, Pharmaceutical, Food, Telecommunication)

 1.Abstract:

This use case considers a tire manufacturing environment in which finished tires moves down through a conveyor channel to a warehouse staging area. Within the staging area, multiple robotic units are installed to automatically pick the individual tires from the conveyor and load them onto pallets. The robotic palletizing process continues until the pallet reaches its predefined capacity limit. Once the pallet is fully loaded, it is transferred to the end of the staging area where it becomes available for warehouse handling operations. A warehouse operator subsequently retrieves the completed pallet and executes the put away process within the warehouse management system, ensuring proper storage and inventory registration.

The overall system landscape consists of four primary systems: SAP ECC, SAP S/4HANA with decentralized Extended Warehouse Management (EWM), the Manufacturing Execution System (MES), and the Cimcorp warehouse automation system. SAP PI/PO serves as the middleware layer to enable communication and data exchange between MES and the decentralized S/4HANA EWM system. This document presents a structured architecture describing the integration and interaction among the aforementioned systems.

Above architecture works well with event driven integration model, which ensures the transactional consistency between MES, CIMPRO and EWM system during whole tire palletization process.

2.0 Introduction:

Example: The tire production line operates at a very high speed and runs continuously 24×7 at full capacity. On average, one tire moves to the staging area every two seconds, resulting in approximately 30 tires per minute. Since the staging area has limited space, automation becomes essential to handle the high production rate efficiently. Without automated handling and palletizing systems, tires would quickly accumulate in the staging area, leading to operational congestion and potential disruption of the production flow.

Another approach would be going without structured integration, robotic systems often operate as isolated system, which restricts real-time inventory visibility and reconciliation challenges between production systems and Warehouse system.

This case study proposes a standardized integration framework connecting a Cimcorp robotic palletizing system with a Manufacturing Execution System (MES) and SAP S/4HANA integrated with embedded SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM). This architecture enables the following:

• Real-time pallets build confirmation
• Pallet handling unit creation
• Serial-level traceability for tiers
• Goods receipt automation
• Warehouse bin put-away
• End-to-end production-to-warehouse synchronization

The proposed design supports scalable automation environments commonly found in modern tire manufacturing plants.

3.0 System Architecture:

a. Enterprise Resource Planning Layer (ERP Layer)

This layer manages enterprise-wide business processes and master data.

Systems:

  • SAP ECC – Legacy ERP system responsible for financials, material master data, sales orders, and enterprise reporting.

Key Responsibilities

  • Material master and business master data management
  • Financial and sales transactions
  • Inventory visibility at enterprise level
  • Integration with warehouse and production systems

b. Warehouse Management Layer

This layer handles warehouse execution and logistics operations.

System:

  • SAP S/4HANA with Decentralized Extended Warehouse Management (EWM)

Key Responsibilities

  • Warehouse task creation and management
  • Inventory tracking and stock movements
  • Put away and storage bin management
  • Handling unit and pallet management
  • Integration with warehouse automation systems

c. Manufacturing Execution Layer

This layer manages shop-floor production control and operational execution.

System:

  • Manufacturing Execution System (MES)

Key Responsibilities

  • Production order execution
  • Batch and lot tracking
  • Shop-floor monitoring and control
  • Production reporting and traceability
  • Communication with warehouse and automation systems

d. Warehouse Control and Automation Layer

This layer controls physical automation equipment on the warehouse floor.

System:

  • Cimcorp Warehouse Control System (WCS)

Key Responsibilities

  • Control of robotic palletizing systems
  • Conveyor and buffer management
  • Pallet creation and movement
  • Real-time equipment monitoring
  • Communication with MES for production and pallet data
  • (Note: although PI/PO is not the best option for S4HANA but as PI/PO was already using in ECC application, so used this one here)

4.0 Interesting facts about Tire:

Size of tire, which is imposed on tire physically, which is very much visible to all. This help to understand the exact width, Aspect ratio and Rim diameter for each tire.

 

 

And below summary, shows tire types, main features and major manufactures.

5.0 Event-Driven Integration Model:

5.1 Pallet build lifecycle:

5.1.1 Assumptions:

  • Each robot has a unique robot number and dedicated staging area
    • Each pallet has a unique pallet HU identifier
    • Each tire has a unique serial number encoded as a HU
    • Tiers with identical characteristics share the same material number
    • Each pallet has a predefined capacity stored within MES and WCS logic

5.1.2 Palletization steps:

 

Step 1: Tier Arrival Detection

A sensor or vision system detects tire arrival. The robot scans the pallet identifier and the tire serial number before pallet placement.

Step 2: Robotic Pallet Placement

The robot places tiers on the pallet following a predefined stacking pattern.

Step 3: WCS Event Recording

The WCS records the pallet build information including:

  • Pallet ID
    • Serial number(tyre serial)
    • Material number
    • Date and time
    • Quantity
  • Stock Type
  • Date of manufacturing
  • Date of expiry

 

Step 4: Pallet Completion

Once the pallet reaches the defined capacity, the robot moves the pallet to an outbound conveyor staging area. And then WCS send MES, pallet information and following this, MES updates this record and complete order. marks the pallet as complete in their record.

 

Step 5: XML Message Transmission

  • The pallet information is transmitted to MES and SAP S/4HANA using an XML interface. In this scenario, we are using the Push mechanism, wherein MES is sending Pallet file in XML format though middle layer(SAP PI/PO) then respective pallet information is saved in a file under Input folder.
  • From MES side: Once XML file goes to SAP/PI/PO then that file, moves from process folder to achieve folder
  • As soon as XML file saves in file under SAP, then parallel information goes to queue(can verify in SMQ2/SMQ1) and process the each pallet.

 

Example:

Step 6: Tire Handling Unit Creation

In SAP, each tire serial number is created as an individual handling unit with expiration characteristics (Added below fields in /SCWM/HUITM)

Step 7: Pallet Handling Unit Creation

A pallet handling unit is generated, and the tire handling units are packed under the pallet. This step will develop a parent child relation between Tire(HU) and Pallet(HU). Once one pallet is processed then that file will move from Inbound to archive folder.

Assumptions:

  • Each tire serial number is unique and HU is presenting the same in SAP
  • HU is also presenting Pallet in SAP.
  • Number range of both pallets are different to make this very clear to everyone.

Step 8: Warehouse Put-Away

The pallet handling unit is automatically put away into the warehouse storage bin using SAP EWM warehouse task processing.

Below is example, how stock looks like(with pallet HU & Child HU):

 

 

6.0 Error handling in S4HANA:

1)At any point of time, if someone want to check, that whether/how many XML messages received in S4HANA, which can be verified by using tcode : SXMB_MONI

As plant is working on 24 x 7then messages should come very frequently like 1-2 minutes, if it is not then need to check interface.

 

 

 

2)In case tire serial(Tire HU) number already exist then this pallet(HU) would be in error queue. Someone need to check the queue in frequent interval to avoid the errors.

3)Sometime, if XML is missing some information like stock type or quantity or some field is blank, then also pallet would be in queue error.

4) Sometime, if XML is populating wrong material number, missing some information like stock type or quantity or some field is blank, then also pallet would be in queue error.

7.0 Data Objects and Traceability:

The integration synchronizes both master and transactional data objects. This structure ensures complete traceability from production line to warehouse storage.

Master Data

  • Material master (material number of Tire type): There is mapping table for MES material number and SAP material/Product number.
    • Storage location(need to correctly align with storage type/Availability group)
    • Warehouse bins

Transactional Data

  • Handling unit (Tire serial number)
    • Handling unit (Pallet)
    • Warehouse stock

1.There is a mapping between MES stock type and SAP EWM stock type:

MES

EWM

Free      

P2(Unrestricted)

Blocked

B2(Blocked)

8.0 Reliability and Transactional Integrity:

To ensure robust system operation, the integration framework implements the following safeguards.

  • Idempotent message processing to prevent duplicate postings
    • Store-and-forward queuing in MES and Cimcorp to tolerate ERP downtime
    • Error quarantine handling for label scanning failures or quantity mismatches
    • Periodic reconciliation between MES pallet records and SAP inventory postings

These mechanisms ensure consistent synchronization between shop-floor events and enterprise inventory records

9.0 Conclusion:

This Use case, integration of a robotic tire palletizing cell with Cimcorp automation, MES, and SAP S/4HANA establishes a digitally synchronized production-to-warehouse workflow. By implementing this event-driven architecture and structured handling unit management, the system provides:

  • Real-time inventory visibility – As soon Tire is manufactured in production, with the help of palletization, we can easily check the inventory.
    • Serial-level (HU based) traceability each product - At Tire HU level Manufacturing date, expiry date, country of origin. If product is manufactured in multiple locations - At tire level, one can easily identify, where tire was manufactured. One can easily identify the expiry date of each tire, which will help to restrict to dispatch the expired tire to customer.
    • Automated warehouse execution specially in palletization - Automated warehouse execution, particularly in palletization, plays a key role in modern manufacturing and distribution environments. In such systems, finished goods coming from production lines are automatically arranged and stacked onto pallets using robotic palletizers. Sensors and conveyor systems transport the products to palletizing stations where robots organize them according to predefined stacking patterns to ensure stability and optimal space utilization.
    • Scalable automation integration - Scalable automation integration enables warehouses and manufacturing facilities to expand their automation capabilities as operational demands grow. By designing systems with modular and flexible architectures, organizations can easily add new equipment such as conveyors, robotic palletizers, without disrupting existing operations. Integration with enterprise platforms like SAP Extended Warehouse Management ensures that automated equipment can communicate seamlessly with warehouse processes, inventory management, and order fulfilment activities. This approach supports real-time data exchange, efficient material flow control, and centralized monitoring of automated systems.

entry.title: Re: Setting Up Alerts for SAP Cloud Integration Using SAP Alert Notification Service
entry.content:
Hi @Nikhil_Gursal,

Thank you for sharing the detailed steps to enable the SAP BTP Alert Notification Service and configure the Cloud Integration iFlow to trigger alerts.

I have followed the steps as described in the blog; however, I am currently stuck with the following error:

Error Details
com.sap.gateway.core.ip.component.odata.exception.OsciException: Error in retrieving Authorization header, cause: java.lang.IllegalArgumentException: Status code:400; Reason:{"error":"invalid_request","error_description":"Missing grant type"}

I have configured the grant_type=client_credentials in the security material.

I have maintained the credentials for both the Process Integration Runtime service and the Alert Notification Service. I also tried using basic authentication, but the issue persists.

Could you please suggest any additional checks or steps I can perform to resolve this issue?

Best regards,
Bhargava Krishna

entry.title: COR6N: Batch Selection F4 Help Displays Stock in Base UoM Instead of Document Alternate UoM
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

When performing a process order confirmation in transaction COR6N, the batch selection F4 help (Search Help) displays the unrestricted stock quantity in the Base Unit of Measure (Base UoM) even when the production order and component requirements are maintained in an Alternate Unit of Measure (Alt UoM).

Steps to Reproduce
1. Maintain Material Master: Create a material with a Base UoM (e.g., KG) and an Alternate UoM (e.g., L).
2. Create Process Order: Create and release a process order where the component requirement is in the Alternate UoM (L).
3. Execute COR6N: Go to transaction COR6N, enter the order number, and navigate to the Goods Movements screen.
4. Trigger F4 Help: Place the cursor on the 'Batch' field for the component and press F4 to see available stock.
5. Observe Quantities: View the "Unrestricted Stock" column in the resulting popup.

Expected result
stock quantities in the Alternate Unit of Measure

But actual results we are getting
tock quantities only in the Base Unit of Measure

Any insights on how to achieve this in public cloud😁.

Thank you!

Best regards,
Kay


##SAP Production Planning #SAP PP-PI

entry.title: How to Create an Object List Page in SAP Fiori Elements – Step-by-Step Guide
entry.content:
Introduction : SAP Fiori Elements provides predefined floorplans that help developers build enterprise applications quickly with minimal coding. One of the most commonly used floorplans is the Object Page. The Object Page is designed to display detailed information about a single business object in a structured and organized layout. It allows users to view, edit, and manage detailed data while navigating through different sections of related information within the same page.

SAP Fiori Elements Object Page :

Object Page =  A detailed screen that shows complete information about a selected business object.

When a user selects one record from a List Report, the system navigates to the Object Page to show the full details.

Flow of  Analytical List Page:

  1. CDS View
  2. Annotation
  3. Odata service
  4. SAP Fiori Elements ( Analytical List Page) Template
  5. SAP Fiori Application UI

Step 1: Create CDS View and Metadata Extension:

First, create the Core Data Services (CDS) view that exposes the required business data.
After creating the CDS view, create a Metadata Extension and add the necessary UI annotations to define how the data should appear on the SAP Fiori Elements Object Page.

The metadata extension is used to separate the UI annotations from the main CDS view, which helps maintain a cleaner and more structured development approach.

Note: It is assumed that you already know how to create a CDS View and a Metadata Extension in the ABAP environment. Therefore, this article does not cover the step-by-step creation process. Instead, the examples below directly demonstrate the CDS view and Metadata Extension with the required annotations used for the SAP Fiori Elements Object Page.

1. CDS View:

Code:

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZOLR'
@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true
@AbapCatalog.preserveKey: true
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Object List Report'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true
define view ZCDS_ObjectList_Report
  as select from ztest_sales_tab
{
  key vbeln as SalesDocument,

      vkorg as SalesOrg,
      werks as Plant,
      vtweg as DistrChannel,
      @DefaultAggregation: #SUM
      netwr as Amount,
      waerk as Currency,
      audat as CreationDate,
      case substring( audat, 5, 2 )
      when '01' then concat( 'JAN-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '02' then concat( 'FEB-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '03' then concat( 'MAR-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '04' then concat( 'APR-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '05' then concat( 'MAY-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '06' then concat( 'JUN-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '07' then concat( 'JUL-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '08' then concat( 'AUG-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '09' then concat( 'SEP-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '10' then concat( 'OCT-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '11' then concat( 'NOV-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '12' then concat( 'DEC-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )

      end   as Month_Year,
       'Purchase Order Title '              as sampleTitle,
      'My Purchase Order for Goods Services' as sampleDes
    
}

2. Meta data Extension:

Code:

@Metadata.layer: #CORE
@Search.searchable: true


@UI.headerInfo: {
 typeName: 'Purchase Order',
 typeNamePlural: 'Purchase OrderS',
 title: {  type: #STANDARD, value: 'sampleTitle' },
 description:{ type: #STANDARD, value: 'sampleDes' }
 //imageUrl: 'image1'

}


annotate view ZCDS_ObjectList_Report with



{


   .facet: [{
          id: 'General',
          purpose: #STANDARD,
          label: 'Purchase Order',
          type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE
      }]
      
  .defaultSearchElement: true
  .lineItem: [{position: 10}]
  .selectionField: [{ position: 10 }]
  .identification: [{position: 10  }]
  SalesDocument;
  .lineItem: [{position: 20}]
  .selectionField: [{ position: 20 }]
  .identification: [{position: 20  }]
  SalesOrg;
  .lineItem: [{position: 30}]
  .selectionField: [{ position: 30 }]
  .identification: [{position: 30  }]
  Plant;
  .lineItem: [{position: 40}]
  .identification: [{position: 40  }]
  DistrChannel;
  .lineItem: [{position: 50}]
  .identification: [{position: 50  }]
  .dataPoint: { title: 'Amount' , criticality: 'CAmount'}
  Amount;
  .lineItem: [{position: 60}]
  .identification: [{position: 60  }]
  Currency;
  .lineItem: [{position: 70}]
  .identification: [{position: 70  }]
  CreationDate;

  .lineItem: [{position: 80,label: 'Month Year'}]
  .selectionField: [{ position: 40  }]
  .identification: [{position: 80  }]
  .dataPoint: { title: 'Month & Year' }
  Month_Year;

}

Step 2: Consume the CDS View in an OData Service:

Note: It is assumed that you already know how to consume a CDS View in an OData service. Therefore, this article does not cover the step-by-step process. Instead, the explanation below directly continues with the required configuration and examples.

Go to SEGW  Tcode and open your  Project and  to add your CDS View .

After adding the CDS entity, the Entity Type, Entity Set, and related properties will be generated automatically in the project.

Step 3:  Create SAP Fiori Application using Object Page Template

Follow the steps  :

Open  BAS Tool and  Click on File and Select as New Project from Template  and   chose  List Report Page template .Please provide the necessary details and Finish It.

After completing these steps, the system automatically generates the SAP Fiori Elements Object Page application.

Once all the above steps are completed and the configuration is done, preview the application in SAP Business Application Studio. The application will load, and you will be able to see the Object List Page with the data displayed.

After completing all the configuration steps, preview the application in SAP Business Application Studio. The application opens with the Object List Page, which displays the list of records in a tabular format.

The Object List Page provides the following features:

  • Filter Bar to search data using fields such as:

    • Sales Document

    • Sales Organization

    • Plant

    • Month_Year

  • Table View displaying all the records retrieved from the OData service.

  • Navigation Arrow on each row for accessing detailed information.

Navigation to Object Page :

Each row in the table contains a navigation arrow ( > ) on the right side.

When you click the arrow icon, the application navigates to the Object Page, where detailed information about the selected record is displayed

Example:

List Report → Object Page

5000000001 > opens detailed view

The Object Page shows the complete information of the selected Sales Document.

 

Conclusion: In conclusion, the Object Page in SAP Fiori Elements provides a structured way to display and manage detailed information about a specific business object. By organizing data into sections and enabling actions such as viewing and editing, it improves usability and helps users efficiently work with business data within a single interface.

Thank You,

B Naresh Kumar Nayak

entry.title: Re: Run Datasphere Task Chains from SAC Multi-Actions via External API Step
entry.content:
Hi @Sebastian_Gesiarz 

There can be some latency since multi-actions uses polling mechanism to query the status of remote task chain, and the default interval of 2 queries can be 10s or so. Thus an average delay of several seconds up to 10+ seconds can be regarded as expected.  If you encountered much severer delays consistently, that can be something to check, and I haven't experienced that on my side yet.

Thanks.

entry.title: Re: Transport changes not available in QA but successful...
entry.content:
At this stage we are leaning towards a buffer issue.

After running $sync we were able to import transports into QA successfully and changes updated.

The issue returned and we did a restart of the complete system. Success for 2 days.
